nadia_lin.name: Nadia Lin
nadia_lin.summary:
Nadia Lin is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U17 level, with 8 entries across 3 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Nadia is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Nadia has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
nadia_lin.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nadia Lin, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 8 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Nadia is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 NE MassBad OLC, and so far, the only thing Nadia has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Nadia saga began at 2024 NE MassBad OLC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nadia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 8 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Nadia has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nadia has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nadia showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Nadia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Nadia entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Nadia paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Nadia keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Nadia has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Nadia, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Nadia. We will be here, taking notes.

nadya_tirtohadi.name: Nadya Tirtohadi
nadya_tirtohadi.summary:
Nadya Tirtohadi is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U19 level, with 12 entries across 6 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Nadya is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

With 5 different doubles partners over the years, Nadya adapts to anyone:
  • Patrick Xiao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, across 2 seasons)

Nadya has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NorCal, SoCal, South, Nadya isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Nadya's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
nadya_tirtohadi.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nadya Tirtohadi, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Nadya's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Nadya keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Nadya saga began at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nadya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Nadya has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Patrick Xiao: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Patrick Xiao has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17 — we hope Patrick Xiao has emotionally recovered.

Nadya has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nadya has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nadya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17, which means Nadya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Nadya entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Nadya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Nadya keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Nadya has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Nadya, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Nadya. We will be here, taking notes.

naksh_shetty.name: Naksh Shetty
naksh_shetty.summary:
Hailing from California, Naksh Shetty has climbed to the top of the mountain at Junior Nationals, the crown jewel of the American junior badminton season in BS U11. That title represents the culmination of grueling competition against the very best in the country. With 15 career victories across 13 tournaments, Naksh has proven the ability to deliver when it matters most.

Notably, Naksh has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Naksh especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 6 singles titles, 4 doubles titles, 5 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals champion (BS U11) • 9 ORC titles.

The statistics paint a vivid picture — Naksh boasts a career +1109 point differential, 12 three-game battles — proving Naksh is no stranger to the pressure of a decider, 56 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively.

With 9 different doubles partners over the years, Naksh adapts to anyone:
  • Xiner Hu (current primary partner) — 8 events together this season alone with 3 titles, building serious momentum (8 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Iniyan Arun (current partner) — an ongoing partnership that keeps producing results: 2 titles across 3 events in BD, across 2 seasons
  • Keaton Ou (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in BD, across 2 seasons

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 4 tournaments. Naksh is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Naksh has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Naksh has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 7 seeded entries across the career, Naksh consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 4 seasons and 15 titles, Naksh has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
naksh_shetty.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Naksh Shetty, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has stood at the top of the podium at Junior Nationals — the crown jewel of the American junior badminton calendar — and won BS U11. That is a real, verified, indisputable accomplishment, and no one can ever take it away. But here's what they CAN take away: any illusion that the rest of this resume is equally impressive. Because we are looking at 33 career entries across 13 tournaments, 15 total wins, and 18 losses. A 45% win rate that suggests the Nationals title was less a coronation and more a cosmic accident that Naksh has been unsuccessfully trying to replicate ever since. The story started at 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC, and it has been a wild, inconsistent, deeply roastable ride from there.

The Naksh saga began at 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2024 Dave Freeman Jr OLC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Naksh's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Naksh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 6 singles, 4 doubles, 5 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Naksh can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Naksh has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Naksh's texts. The most successful partnership is with Xiner Hu — 8 events, 3 titles — and honestly, Xiner Hu deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Xiner Hu a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Boqian Zheng has emotionally recovered.

Naksh has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Naksh is not just losing locally — Naksh is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 45% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Shetty family travels cross-country so Naksh can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Naksh has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Naksh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U13, which means Naksh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, where Naksh entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Naksh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 15 titles to the name, 33 entries, and 18 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Naksh looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Naksh's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Naksh has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Naksh. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

namratha_nishal.name: Namratha Nishal
namratha_nishal.summary:
Out of North Carolina, Namratha Nishal has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Namratha's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

What makes Namratha especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (6 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

With 14 different doubles partners over the years, Namratha adapts to anyone:
  • Mithun Gopinath (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (9 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Tsun Him Lam (current partner) — currently teaming up in U15/XD (3 events, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Diyaa Sarath (current partner) — currently teaming up in U15 (2 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Namratha has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Namratha isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Namratha brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
namratha_nishal.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Namratha Nishal, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 42 career entries in 16 tournaments, Namratha has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 41 entries where Namratha drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 42 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Namratha saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13) with 3 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Namratha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 42 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Namratha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Namratha's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Mithun Gopinath: 9 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 9 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Mithun Gopinath has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Mithun Gopinath has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Namratha entered 6 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Namratha's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Namratha and said 'nah.'

Namratha has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Namratha has 24 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Namratha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U15, which means Namratha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Namratha entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Namratha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Namratha keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Namratha has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Namratha, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Namratha. We will be here, taking notes.

nancy_qu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nancy Qu, from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Nancy is right on schedule. It all started at 2021 NW CRC, and so far, the only thing Nancy has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Nancy saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Nancy is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Nancy's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Nancy is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

Nancy is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Nancy wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Nancy, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

nandan_mallela.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nandan Mallela, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Nandan has 21 career entries across 9 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Nandan saga began at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nandan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 21 entries, zero titles in any format. On the doubles front, Nandan has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Jake Shi: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Jake Shi has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Jake Shi has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Nandan has shown up 3 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Nandan is not just losing locally — Nandan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Mallela family travels cross-country so Nandan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nandan has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nandan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Nandan entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Nandan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Nandan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Nandan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Nandan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Nandan. We will be here, taking notes.

On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 7 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Naomi has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Naomi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U19, which means Naomi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip.

Season after season, Naomi keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Naomi has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Naomi, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Naomi. We will be here, taking notes.

naomie_lei.name: Naomie Lei
naomie_lei.summary:
Naomie Lei is just getting started on the junior circuit with 2 tournament entries. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Naomie's.

Naomie competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
naomie_lei.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Naomie Lei, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Naomie is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, and so far, the only thing Naomie has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Naomie saga began at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Naomie has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Naomie showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U15, which means Naomie traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in GD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, where Naomie entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Naomie paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Naomie is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Naomie wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Naomie, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

napat_srisalaipaisal.name: Napat Srisalaipaisal
napat_srisalaipaisal.summary:
Representing Texas, Napat Srisalaipaisal has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Napat's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Napat has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Napat competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (12 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Napat has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 28 different players across the career:
  • Zimeng Zhou (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Coco Cenke Tao (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Napat has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Napat has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 14 seeded entries across the career, Napat consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 1 title, Napat has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
napat_srisalaipaisal.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Napat Srisalaipaisal, from Texas, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 87 career entries in 33 tournaments, Napat has 1 title — a 1% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 86 entries where Napat drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 87 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Napat saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) with 2 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2022 South Houston CRC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Napat's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 20 entries — a stretch where Napat actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Napat is 0-for-10. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Napat experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Napat has reached 8 championship matches across this career. That is 8 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 8 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 South CRC, where Napat entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U17, BD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (5 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Napat does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Napat has never won anything. But 8 finals losses versus 1 wins means Napat is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Napat plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Napat is 0-for-29. Zero titles in 29 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Napat and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Napat needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 28 of them, to be precise. Napat has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Napat's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Riya Suresh: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Riya Suresh has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Riya Suresh has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Napat's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Napat entered 12 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Napat's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Napat and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Napat has shown up 3 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Napat has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Napat is not just losing locally — Napat is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 1% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Srisalaipaisal family travels cross-country so Napat can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Napat has 44 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Napat showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U15, which means Napat traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 NW ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Napat entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Napat paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 87 entries, and 86 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Napat looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Napat's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Napat has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Napat. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Pradyun Rasamsetti has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in BD U17 — we hope Pradyun Rasamsetti has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Nathan's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Nathan entered 2 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Nathan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Nathan and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Nathan has competed 8 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one.

Nathan has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nathan has 22 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nathan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U17, which means Nathan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Nathan entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Nathan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 39 entries, and 37 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Nathan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Nathan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Nathan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Nathan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

nathan_chu.name: Nathan Chu
nathan_chu.summary:

nathan_chu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nathan Chu, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 10 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 10-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Nathan's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Nathan keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Nathan saga began at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC with 1 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nathan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 10 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Nathan has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nathan has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nathan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U15, which means Nathan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 Midwest ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, where Nathan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Nathan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Nathan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Nathan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Nathan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Nathan. We will be here, taking notes.

If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Nathan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Nathan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Nathan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Nathan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

nathan_ong.name: Nathan Ong
nathan_ong.summary:
A Washington competitor, Nathan Ong is a consistent semifinalist, currently competing at the U17 level, reaching the final four 3 times. That kind of reliability at the top of the draw is the signature of a player who will soon be contending for titles.

What makes Nathan especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Nathan has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Isaac Wong (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events)

Nathan has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Nathan's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
nathan_ong.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nathan Ong, from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Nathan's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Nathan keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Nathan saga began at 2024 SBC NW CRC with 2 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nathan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Nathan has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nathan has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nathan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Nathan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, where Nathan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Nathan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Nathan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Nathan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Nathan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Nathan. We will be here, taking notes.

nathaniel_volfbeyn.name: Nathaniel Volfbeyn
nathaniel_volfbeyn.summary:
Hailing from Washington, Nathaniel Volfbeyn has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Nathaniel's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Nathaniel is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Nathaniel competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 2 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Nathaniel has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 22 different players across the career:
  • Iris Kuan (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (6 total events in XD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Daniel Bicheng Fong (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #5)
  • Katherine F Xu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Nathaniel has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Nathaniel has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 8 seeded entries across the career, Nathaniel consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 5 titles, Nathaniel is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
nathaniel_volfbeyn.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nathaniel Volfbeyn, from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 90 career entries in 32 tournaments, Nathaniel has 5 titles — a 6% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 17 entries where Nathaniel drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 90 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Nathaniel saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC in BS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Nathaniel's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 2 titles in 22 entries — a stretch where Nathaniel actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Nathaniel is 0-for-15. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Nathaniel experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Nathaniel has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 Midwest ORC, where Nathaniel entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U13, BD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Nathaniel does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Nathaniel has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 5 wins means Nathaniel is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nathaniel plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 2 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Nathaniel can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Nathaniel has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Nathaniel's texts. The go-to partner is William Geng (12 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Anya Kuan has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Nathaniel's 5 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Nathaniel entered 3 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Nathaniel's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Nathaniel and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Nathaniel has shown up 9 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6. Tournament committees have handed Nathaniel a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Nathaniel has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2024 Dave Freeman Jr OLC in BS U15, where Nathaniel was seeded #1 and finished 5th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

One thing nobody can accuse Nathaniel of is playing it safe. Nathaniel has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Nathaniel has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 5 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Nathaniel has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Nathaniel is not just losing locally — Nathaniel is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Volfbeyn family travels cross-country so Nathaniel can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nathaniel has 44 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nathaniel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15, which means Nathaniel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Nathaniel entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Nathaniel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 90 entries, and 85 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Nathaniel looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Nathaniel's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Nathaniel has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Nathaniel. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

navya_saxena.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Navya Saxena, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 4 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Navya is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 Midwest CRC, and so far, the only thing Navya has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Navya saga began at 2022 Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Navya has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Navya has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Navya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Navya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 Midwest CRC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Navya entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Navya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Navya is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Navya wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Navya, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

neal_vijay.name: Neal Vijay
neal_vijay.summary:
Representing Illinois, Neal Vijay has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U15 level, breaking into the semifinals 2 times. Across 12 entries in 6 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

A true triple-threat, Neal competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Neal has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Neal isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Neal's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
neal_vijay.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Neal Vijay, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Neal's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Neal keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Neal saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Neal plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Neal has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Chuhan Michelle Liu has emotionally recovered.

Neal has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Neal has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Neal showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U15, which means Neal traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Neal entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Neal paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Neal keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Neal has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Neal, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Neal. We will be here, taking notes.

neel_achari.name: Neel Achari
neel_achari.summary:
Representing Texas, Neel Achari has conquered the Open Regional Championship stage, capturing an impressive 4 ORC titles across 2 regional championships. ORCs are the highest level of regular-season competition, drawing the strongest fields in each region. Winning at this level repeatedly marks Neel as one of the premier players on the circuit.

Notably, Neel has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Neel competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 3 singles titles, 3 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries) • 4 ORC titles.

Beyond the trophies, the numbers stand out: Neel boasts a biggest win margin of 36 points, a career +962 point differential, a solid 68% win rate over 108 matches.

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Neel adapts to anyone:
  • Harry H Song (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (4 total events in BD)
  • Adalyn Puah (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2)
  • Devaansh Surpur (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in BD, across 2 seasons
  • Pradhyumn Surendran (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 6 titles across 8 tournaments — a clear sign that Neel is still ascending.

Neel has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Neel has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seeded entries across the career, Neel consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Neel's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
neel_achari.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Neel Achari, from Texas, currently competing at the U11 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Neel has 40 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 6 of them — a 15% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Neel saga began at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2025 Austin Leander South OLC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Neel's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 6 titles in 23 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Neel experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Neel has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Neel does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Neel has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 6 wins means Neel is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Neel plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 3 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Neel can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Neel has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Neel's texts. The go-to partner is Vihaa Reddy Keesara (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11 — we hope Vihaa Reddy Keesara has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Neel has shown up 2 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9. Tournament committees have handed Neel a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Neel has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2025 Fortius South CRC in BS U13, where Neel was seeded #2 and finished 5th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Neel has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Neel is not just losing locally — Neel is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 15% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Achari family travels cross-country so Neel can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Neel has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Neel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U11, which means Neel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Neel entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Neel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 40 entries, and 34 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Neel looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Neel's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Neel has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Neel. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

neel_auro_chandra.roast:

That means for every win, there are about 8 entries where Neel drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 18 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Neel saga began at 2022 South Frisco ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The longest-running partnership is with Matthew Jing: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Matthew Jing has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U19 — we hope Matthew Jing has emotionally recovered.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 U.S. Selection Event in U19 BS, which means Neel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 U.S. Selection Event in U19 BS. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 18 entries, and 16 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Neel looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Neel's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Neel has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Neel. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
