A true triple-threat, Neel competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (7 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries).

Neel has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Neel has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience, Neel brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
neel_j_reddy.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Neel J Reddy, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 52 career entries in 25 tournaments, Neel has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 51 entries where Neel drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 52 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Neel saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 6 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Neel plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 52 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Neel has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Neel's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Samiksh Talapally: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Samiksh Talapally has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Ashni Mathuré has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Neel entered 7 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Neel's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Neel and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Neel has shown up 9 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Neel is not just losing locally — Neel is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Reddy family travels cross-country so Neel can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Neel has 33 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Neel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U13, which means Neel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Neel entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Neel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 6 seasons, 52 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Neel. Neel just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Neel has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Neel. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Neel: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

neha_kulkarni.name: Neha Kulkarni
neha_kulkarni.summary:
Out of Illinois, Neha Kulkarni is a multi-time champion on the junior circuit, currently competing at the U19 level, capturing 3 titles across 9 tournaments and 18 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Neha keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Neha is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Neha especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 2 singles titles, 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior Championships competitor (2025) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Neha has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Anvi Sai Harish — 4 events, 1 title together in GD, across 2 seasons

Neha is peaking at the right time in 2024-2025, with 1 title across 2 tournaments — a clear sign that Neha is still ascending.

Neha has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Over 5 seasons and 3 titles, Neha has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
neha_kulkarni.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Neha Kulkarni, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. Hold on — Neha has competed at the Pan American Junior Championships. That is an international tournament where you literally represent your country. Sounds amazing, right? Except Neha finished with a best of top-9, which means Neha flew to another country, wore the red-white-and-blue, stepped on court as a representative of the United States... and got bounced before the podium was even set up. Patriotism is beautiful. The results were not. Moving on to the other 16 career entries.

The Neha saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 13th. The first title would not come until 2023 Midwest OLC in GS U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Neha's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 2 titles in 3 entries — a stretch where Neha actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 3 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Neha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Neha can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Neha's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Neha has shown up 3 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Neha has also played up into older age groups at 3 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Neha has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Neha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U17, which means Neha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-25 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Neha entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Neha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 18 entries, and 15 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Neha looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Neha's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Neha has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Neha. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Neil competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
neil_feihin_shilagani.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Neil FeiHin Shilagani, from New York, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Neil is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, and so far, the only thing Neil has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Neil saga began at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Neil has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Neil showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U13, which means Neil traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, where Neil entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Neil paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Neil is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Neil wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Neil, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

neil_ganguly.name: Neil Ganguly
neil_ganguly.summary:
Representing Illinois, Neil Ganguly is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Neil in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

What makes Neil especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #2, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U19 BD) • Junior Nationals top-2 (XD U17, BD U17) • 1 ORC title.

With 6 different doubles partners over the years, Neil adapts to anyone:
  • Michael Xu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U19 (10 events, best: #2, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Angie Huang (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (6 total events in U19/XD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Andrea Li — 4 events, 1 title together in XD, across 2 seasons

Neil has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Neil has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 10 seeded entries across the career, Neil consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Neil is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
neil_ganguly.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Neil Ganguly, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. Top-4 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 36 career entries in 13 tournaments, Neil has 2 titles — a 6% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 17 entries where Neil drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 36 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Neil saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2022 Midwest CRC in XD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Neil's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 16 entries — a stretch where Neil actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Neil is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Neil experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Neil has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Neil entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U17, BD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. Now yes, Neil does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Neil has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 2 wins means Neil is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Neil plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Neil is 0-for-10. Zero titles in 10 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Neil and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Neil needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Neil has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Michael Xu: 10 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 10 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Michael Xu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U19 — we hope Michael Xu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Neil entered 8 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Neil's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Neil and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has been particularly cruel: 3 entries, 2 finals appearances, and zero titles. Neil has stood on the biggest stage in American junior badminton, one match from the most important title in the sport, and come up short 2 times. That is the kind of heartbreak that builds character — or destroys it. Time will tell. Then there is the seeding situation. Neil has been seeded in 10 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Neil takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, and finishing 13th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Neil. The evidence suggests they should not have.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Neil is not just losing locally — Neil is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ganguly family travels cross-country so Neil can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Neil has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Neil showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U19, which means Neil traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Neil entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 21st, which is a polite way of saying Neil paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 36 entries, and 34 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Neil looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Neil's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Neil has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Neil. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

nethmi_hewawitharana.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nethmi Hewawitharana, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 21 events across 8 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 21-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Nethmi's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Nethmi keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Nethmi saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Nethmi has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Arushi Panda (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17 — we hope Shaurya Agrawal has emotionally recovered.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nethmi has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nethmi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Nethmi entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Nethmi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Nethmi keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Nethmi has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Nethmi, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Nethmi. We will be here, taking notes.

netra_h_shetty.name: Netra H Shetty
netra_h_shetty.summary:
Netra H Shetty is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist, earning a podium finish representing the USA against the best young players in the Western Hemisphere (GS U17). A top-3 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Netra in rarified international company.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior bronze medalist (GS U17, 2019).
netra_h_shetty.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Netra H Shetty, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U17 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Netra is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist (GS U17), which means Netra has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Netra's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a bronze medal: it means Netra went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Netra represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 1 career entries and a 0% win rate, the Pan Am bronze is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 1 entries worth of material.

The Netra saga began at 2019 Pan Am Junior with 1 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Netra is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Netra's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Netra is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

Netra is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Netra wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. nevaan_bhanot.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nevaan Bhanot, from Virginia, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 24 events across 12 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 24-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Nevaan's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Nevaan keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Nevaan saga began at 2023 South OLC (TBTT) with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nevaan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 24 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Nevaan has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Niranjan Rajasekaran: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Niranjan Rajasekaran has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19) in BD U11 — we hope Niranjan Rajasekaran has emotionally recovered.

Nevaan has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nevaan has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nevaan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13, which means Nevaan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19) in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Nevaan entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 37th, which is a polite way of saying Nevaan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Nevaan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Nevaan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Nevaan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Nevaan. We will be here, taking notes.

nicholas_chiu.name: Nicholas Chiu
nicholas_chiu.summary:
Hailing from Oregon, Nicholas Chiu has conquered the Open Regional Championship stage, currently competing at the U19 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

What makes Nicholas especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 2 singles titles, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior Championships competitor (2021) • U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • 2 ORC titles.

With 9 different doubles partners over the years, Nicholas adapts to anyone:
  • Nikhil Vasudeva (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U19 (3 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Angela Song (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in XD

The 2021-2022 season tells a story of acceleration, with 4 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Nicholas is still ascending.

With tournaments spanning NE, NW, SoCal, Nicholas isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Nicholas's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
nicholas_chiu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nicholas Chiu, from Oregon, currently competing at the U19 level. Hold on — Nicholas has competed at the Pan American Junior Championships. That is an international tournament where you literally represent your country. Sounds amazing, right? Except Nicholas finished with a best of top-5, which means Nicholas flew to another country, wore the red-white-and-blue, stepped on court as a representative of the United States... and got bounced before the podium was even set up. Patriotism is beautiful. The results were not. Moving on to the other 16 career entries.

The Nicholas saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 7th. The first title would not come until 2021 NW CRC in BS U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Nicholas's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2021-2022 season has actually been the best yet — 4 titles in 15 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nicholas plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 1 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Nicholas can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Nicholas has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Nicholas's texts. The go-to partner is Nikhil Vasudeva (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U19 — we hope Codey Ma has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Nicholas entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Nicholas's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Nicholas and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Nicholas has shown up 3 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Nicholas has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nicholas has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nicholas showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U19, which means Nicholas traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Nicholas entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 17th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Nicholas paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 18 entries, and 14 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Nicholas looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Nicholas's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Nicholas has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Nicholas. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

nicholas_w_lai.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nicholas W Lai, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Nicholas has 6 career entries across 2 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Nicholas saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 13th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nicholas plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 6 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At Junior Nationals, Nicholas has shown up 3 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nicholas has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nicholas showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U19, which means Nicholas traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Nicholas entered 3 events with results ranging from 13th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 21st, which is a polite way of saying Nicholas paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Nicholas keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Nicholas has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Nicholas, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Nicholas. We will be here, taking notes.

nicole_krawczyk.name: Nicole Krawczyk
nicole_krawczyk.summary:
A California competitor, Nicole Krawczyk is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist, earning a podium finish representing the USA against the best young players in the Western Hemisphere (Women's Singles, GS U19, Mixed Doubles). A top-3 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Nicole in rarified international company.

Nicole is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (8 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Nicole is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 4 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior bronze medalist (Women's Singles, GS U19, Mixed Doubles, 2024, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event champion (U15 GS, U19 GS, U17 GD, U17 XD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (GS U19) • 8 ORC titles.

Nicole has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 12 different players across the career:
  • Jun Heng Lai (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (3 total events in Mixed/U19/XD)
  • Emma J. Wei (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #4)
  • Kevin Zhou — 2 events in XD, best: #4
  • Bettie Huang — 2 events, 1 title together in GD

The 2024-2025 season has been outstanding, with 7 podium finishes across 4 tournaments. Nicole is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Nicole has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Nicole has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Nicole has been seeded #1 or #2 in 6 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 5 seasons and 11 titles, Nicole has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
nicole_krawczyk.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nicole Krawczyk, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Nicole is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist (Women's Singles, GS U19, Mixed Doubles), which means Nicole has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Nicole's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a bronze medal: it means Nicole went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Nicole represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 59 career entries and a 19% win rate, the Pan Am bronze is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 59 entries worth of material.

The Nicole saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2021 HPBC NW ORC in GS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Nicole's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 4 titles in 20 entries — a stretch where Nicole actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 9 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Nicole experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Nicole has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Nicole does own 11 actual titles, so it is not like Nicole has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 11 wins means Nicole is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nicole plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 4 singles, 2 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Nicole can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Nicole has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Nicole's texts. The go-to partner is Rocky Yang (15 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in GD U17 — we hope Ella Mak has emotionally recovered.

Then there is the seeding situation. Nicole has been seeded in 10 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Nicole takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Selection Event in U19 GS, and finishing 17th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Nicole. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Nicole of is playing it safe. Nicole has entered older age groups at 8 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Nicole has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 7 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Nicole has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Nicole is not just losing locally — Nicole is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 19% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Krawczyk family travels cross-country so Nicole can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-17 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nicole has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nicole showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in GD U17, which means Nicole traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 U.S. Selection Event in U15 XD. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 11 titles to the name, 59 entries, and 48 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Nicole looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Nicole's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Nicole has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Nicole. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Nidhi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Nidhi is 0-for-32. Zero titles in 32 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Nidhi and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Nidhi needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 24 of them, to be precise. Nidhi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Nidhi's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Tatthva Sharma: 12 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 12 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Tatthva Sharma has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Aryan Mohan has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Nidhi entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Nidhi's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Nidhi and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Nidhi has shown up 12 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9. Then there is the seeding situation. Nidhi has been seeded in 7 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Nidhi takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2022 Dave Freeman OLC in GS U13, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Nidhi. The evidence suggests they should not have.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Nidhi is not just losing locally — Nidhi is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Sujin family travels cross-country so Nidhi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nidhi has 46 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nidhi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Nidhi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Nidhi entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Nidhi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 100 entries, and 98 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Nidhi looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Nidhi's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Nidhi has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Nidhi. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

nihal_kadamba.name: Nihal Kadamba
nihal_kadamba.summary:
A California competitor, Nihal Kadamba has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U15 BD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 8 career titles across 28 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Notably, Nihal has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Nihal especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 3 singles titles, 4 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U15 BD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (BS U17) • 5 ORC titles.

Nihal has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Justin Kan (current primary partner) — 8 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (23 total events in BD/U15/U17/U19, spanning 5 seasons)
  • Vivek Rai (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Nihal is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 1 title across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Nihal is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Nihal has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Nihal has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Nihal has been seeded #1 or #2 in 9 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience and 8 titles, Nihal is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
nihal_kadamba.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nihal Kadamba, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Nihal is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Nihal has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Nihal would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Nihal, we kept reading — all 66 entries across 28 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 12% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Nihal held up a trophy, there were roughly 7 other times Nihal walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Nihal saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 13th. The first title would not come until 2022 South Frisco ORC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Nihal's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 4 titles in 20 entries — a stretch where Nihal actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 9 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Nihal experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Nihal has reached 8 championship matches across this career. That is 8 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 8 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (6 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Nihal does own 8 actual titles, so it is not like Nihal has never won anything. But 8 finals losses versus 8 wins means Nihal is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nihal plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 4 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Nihal can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Nihal has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Nihal's texts. The most successful partnership is with Justin Kan — 23 events, 4 titles — and honestly, Justin Kan deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Justin Kan a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U17 — we hope Justin Kan has emotionally recovered.

Then there is the seeding situation. Nihal has been seeded in 19 events and underperformed the seeding 5 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Nihal takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17, and finishing 17th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Nihal. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Nihal has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Nihal is not just losing locally — Nihal is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 12% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Kadamba family travels cross-country so Nihal can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-49 finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nihal has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nihal showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Nihal traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Nihal entered 3 events with results ranging from 13th to 49th. That is an average placement of 26th, which is a polite way of saying Nihal paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 8 titles to the name, 66 entries, and 58 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Nihal looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Nihal's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Nihal has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Nihal. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Nikitha has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Nikitha isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Nikitha brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
nikitha_sri_dinesh_khanna.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nikitha Sri Dinesh Khanna, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Nikitha has 30 career entries across 11 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Nikitha saga began at 2023 South ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Nikitha would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Nikitha has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Nikitha has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Nikitha's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nikitha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 30 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Nikitha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Nikitha's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Sashanth Senthil: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Sashanth Senthil has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Marzuq Mathani has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Nikitha has shown up 6 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Nikitha has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nikitha has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nikitha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U15, which means Nikitha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Nikitha entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Nikitha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Nikitha keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Nikitha has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Nikitha, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Nikitha. We will be here, taking notes.

nila_ramesh.name: Nila Ramesh
nila_ramesh.summary:
Out of Virginia, Nila Ramesh has collected 2 tournament titles, currently competing at the U15 level, capturing 2 titles across 21 tournaments and 41 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Nila keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Notably, Nila has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Nila competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries).

Nila has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 12 different players across the career:
  • Olivia Liu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)

Nila has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Nila has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Nila consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Nila is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
nila_ramesh.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nila Ramesh, from Virginia, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Nila has 41 career entries across 21 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 5% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Nila saga began at 2023 South OLC with 1 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE CRC (NJBC) in GD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Nila's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 20 entries — a stretch where Nila actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Nila is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Nila experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Nila has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 Peak Sports South OLC, where Nila entered 2 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U15, GD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Nila does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Nila has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 2 wins means Nila is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nila plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Nila can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Nila has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Nila's texts. The go-to partner is Ruohan Joy Du (6 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Henry Hongxu Zhang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Nila's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Nila has shown up 5 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Nila has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Nila is not just losing locally — Nila is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ramesh family travels cross-country so Nila can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nila has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nila showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15, which means Nila traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Nila entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 37th, which is a polite way of saying Nila paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 41 entries, and 39 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Nila looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Nila's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Nila has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Nila. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
