A Washington competitor, Nina Kuan stands as an ORC champion, currently competing at the U13 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Notably, Nina has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Nina competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • 1 ORC title.

Beyond the trophies, the numbers stand out: Nina boasts 16 three-game battles — proving Nina is no stranger to the pressure of a decider, 32 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a biggest win margin of 36 points.

Nina has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 16 different players across the career:
  • Kira Chu (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (6 total events in GD, across 2 seasons)
  • Mingyuan Zang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #4)
  • Serena Li (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in GD

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 3 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Nina is still ascending.

Nina has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Nina has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Nina consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Nina brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
nina_kuan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nina Kuan, from Washington, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Nina has 56 career entries across 20 tournaments and has managed to win 4 of them — a 7% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Nina saga began at 2024 Frisco South ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2025 OBA NW OLC in XD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Nina's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 3 titles in 18 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Nina experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Nina has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Nina does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Nina has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 4 wins means Nina is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nina plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Nina can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Nina has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Nina's texts. The go-to partner is Toby Liu (6 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U11 — we hope Kaishi Yang has emotionally recovered.

Of Nina's 4 career titles, 3 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Nina feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At Junior Nationals, Nina has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this. Then there is the seeding situation. Nina has been seeded in 5 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Nina takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC in GS U11, and finishing 5th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Nina. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Nina has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Nina is not just losing locally — Nina is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Kuan family travels cross-country so Nina can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nina has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nina showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U11, which means Nina traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Nina entered 3 events with results ranging from 13th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 26th, which is a polite way of saying Nina paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 56 entries, and 52 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Nina looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Nina's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Nina has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Nina. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

2024-2025.t: 2025 YONEX Dave Freeman Jr. SoCal OLC
2025 YONEX Dave Freeman Jr. SoCal OLC

ningxi_huang.name: Ningxi Huang
ningxi_huang.summary:
Hailing from Minnesota, Ningxi Huang is a tenacious competitor with 3 quarterfinal-or-better finishes, currently competing at the U11 level, building a strong foundation across 4 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

A true triple-threat, Ningxi competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

In doubles, Ningxi has formed effective partnerships:
  • Nathan Huang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Victoria A Liu (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Ningxi's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
ningxi_huang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ningxi Huang, from Minnesota, currently competing at the U11 level. A player who has entered 10 events across 4 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 10-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Ningxi's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Ningxi keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Ningxi saga began at 2023 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ningxi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 10 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ningxi has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ningxi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U11, which means Ningxi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Ningxi entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Ningxi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Ningxi keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Ningxi has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Ningxi, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Ningxi. We will be here, taking notes.

nippurn_chhabra.name: Nippurn Chhabra
nippurn_chhabra.summary: Out of Oregon, Nippurn Chhabra has stepped onto the competitive stage with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Nippurn's.
nippurn_chhabra.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nippurn Chhabra, from Oregon, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Nippurn is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 NW OLC, and so far, the only thing Nippurn has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Nippurn saga began at 2023 NW OLC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Nippurn is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Nippurn's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Nippurn is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nippurn has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nippurn showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2023 NW OLC in BS U15, which means Nippurn traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Nippurn is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Nippurn wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Nippurn, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

niranjan_manjith.name: Niranjan Manjith
niranjan_manjith.summary:
Representing Texas, Niranjan Manjith is relentlessly knocking on the door, currently competing at the U17 level, reaching 3 finals across 35 entries. A player who reaches that many championship matches has all the tools — the breakthrough title is coming.

Niranjan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries).

Niranjan has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Sashanth Senthil (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (12 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Charvi Murali (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (7 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)

Niranjan has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Niranjan has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Niranjan brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.

The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Niranjan has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? So from the bottom of our hearts, Niranjan: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

nirmalya_routray.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nirmalya Routray, from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Nirmalya has 10 career entries across 6 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Nirmalya saga began at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 25th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

At Junior Nationals, Nirmalya has shown up 4 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nirmalya has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nirmalya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15, which means Nirmalya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Nirmalya entered 2 events with results ranging from 25th to 65th. That is an average placement of 45th, which is a polite way of saying Nirmalya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 10 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Nirmalya. Nirmalya just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Nirmalya has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Nirmalya. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Nirmalya: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

nishaant_anumolu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nishaant Anumolu, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 54 career entries in 21 tournaments, Nishaant has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 53 entries where Nishaant drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 54 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Nishaant saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nishaant plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 54 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Nishaant has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Nishaant's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Aadhya Vinod: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Aadhya Vinod has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Namratha Nishal has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Nishaant entered 2 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Nishaant's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Nishaant and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Nishaant has shown up 6 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Nishaant is not just losing locally — Nishaant is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Anumolu family travels cross-country so Nishaant can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nishaant has 42 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nishaant showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U13, which means Nishaant traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Nishaant entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Nishaant paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 54 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Nishaant. Nishaant just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Nishaant has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Nishaant. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Nishaant: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

nishtha_dubey.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nishtha Dubey, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 1 win across 19 entries in 7 tournaments. That is a 5% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Nishtha shows up to a tournament, Nishtha pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Nishtha saga began at 2022 South Frisco ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2023 Midwest OLC in GD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Nishtha's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Nishtha would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Nishtha has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Nishtha has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Nishtha's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nishtha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Nishtha is 0-for-7. Zero titles in 7 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Nishtha and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Nishtha needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Nishtha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Nishtha's texts. The go-to partner is Eli Cui (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in XD U15 — we hope Zeyue Du has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Nishtha's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Nishtha has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Nishtha has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nishtha has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nishtha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in XD U15, which means Nishtha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 South Frisco ORC, where Nishtha entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Nishtha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 19 entries, and 18 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Nishtha looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Nishtha's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Nishtha has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Nishtha. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

nitesh_ramshankar.name: Nitesh Ramshankar
nitesh_ramshankar.summary:
A Ohio competitor, Nitesh Ramshankar is in the early chapters of a competitive journey, currently competing at the U15 level, with 3 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Nitesh competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
nitesh_ramshankar.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nitesh Ramshankar, from Ohio, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Nitesh is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, and so far, the only thing Nitesh has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Nitesh saga began at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC with 1 entries and a best of 65th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Nitesh has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nitesh has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nitesh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U15, which means Nitesh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Nitesh is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Nitesh wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Nitesh, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

nithesh_haribabu.name: Nithesh Haribabu
nithesh_haribabu.summary:
A North Carolina competitor, Nithesh Haribabu has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-3 in BS U17. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Nithesh belongs with the best in the country.

Nithesh is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Nithesh especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 3 singles titles, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (1 entry) • Junior Nationals top-3 (BS U17) • 1 ORC title.

With 16 different doubles partners over the years, Nithesh adapts to anyone:
  • Mark w Wu — 2 events in BD, best: #3, across 2 seasons

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 2 titles across 3 tournaments — a clear sign that Nithesh is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Nithesh has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Nithesh has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 12 seeded entries across the career, Nithesh consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience and 4 titles, Nithesh is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
nithesh_haribabu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nithesh Haribabu, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 45 career entries in 24 tournaments, Nithesh has 4 titles — a 9% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 10 entries where Nithesh drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 45 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Nithesh saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2023 South OLC (TBTT) in XD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Nithesh's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 3 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nithesh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 0 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Nithesh can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Nithesh has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Nithesh's texts. The go-to partner is Anvitha B Ranganna (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Anvitha B Ranganna has emotionally recovered.

Of Nithesh's 4 career titles, 3 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Nithesh feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. Junior Nationals has seen Nithesh 8 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Tournament committees have handed Nithesh a #1 or #2 seed 4 times, and Nithesh has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2024 South CRC (TBTT) in BS U19, where Nithesh was seeded #2 and finished 9th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

One thing nobody can accuse Nithesh of is playing it safe. Nithesh has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Nithesh has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 4 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Nithesh has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Nithesh is not just losing locally — Nithesh is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 9% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Haribabu family travels cross-country so Nithesh can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nithesh has 22 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nithesh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U13, which means Nithesh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Nithesh entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Nithesh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 45 entries, and 41 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Nithesh looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Nithesh's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Nithesh has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Nithesh. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Nithesh does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Nithesh has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 2 wins means Nithesh is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Nithesh has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Nithesh's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Bavish Reddi Dornala: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Bavish Reddi Dornala has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U15 — we hope Bavish Reddi Dornala has emotionally recovered.

It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Nithesh has shown up 3 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

One thing nobody can accuse Nithesh of is playing it safe. Nithesh has entered older age groups at 5 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Nithesh has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 1 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Nithesh has built a permanent residence on that line. Nithesh has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nithesh has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nithesh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Nithesh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Nithesh entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Nithesh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 31 entries, and 29 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Nithesh looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Nithesh's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Nithesh has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Nithesh. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

nithya_padmanabhan.name: Nithya Padmanabhan
nithya_padmanabhan.summary:
A Illinois competitor, Nithya Padmanabhan has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U15 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 15 entries in 6 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

nithya_padmanabhan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nithya Padmanabhan, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 15 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 15-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Nithya's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Nithya keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Nithya saga began at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nithya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 15 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Nithya has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nithya has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nithya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Nithya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Nithya entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Nithya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Nithya keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Nithya has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Nithya, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Nithya. We will be here, taking notes.

A California competitor, Nityaa Jaiswal has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U13 level, reaching the finals 2 times. With 24 entries across 9 tournaments, Nityaa is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

Nityaa is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Nityaa has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NorCal, SoCal, South, Nityaa isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Nityaa's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
nityaa_jaiswal.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nityaa Jaiswal, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 24 events across 9 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 24-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Nityaa's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Nityaa keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Nityaa saga began at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Nityaa experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Nityaa has reached the finals 2 times — at 2022 SoCal Arena CRC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Nityaa has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nityaa plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 24 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Nityaa has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Nityaa's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U11 — we hope Nicolas Dolar has emotionally recovered.

Nityaa has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nityaa has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nityaa showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U11, which means Nityaa traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 SoCal ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Nityaa entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Nityaa paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Nityaa keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Nityaa has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Nityaa, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Nityaa. We will be here, taking notes.

nivedya_s_nair.name: Nivedya S Nair
nivedya_s_nair.summary:
Representing Illinois, Nivedya S Nair is a multi-time champion on the junior circuit, currently competing at the U17 level, capturing 2 titles across 9 tournaments and 21 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Nivedya keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Nivedya is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, 2 doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 5 different doubles partners over the years, Nivedya adapts to anyone:
  • Veronica Ornatsky (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (8 total events in GD, spanning 3 seasons)

Nivedya is peaking at the right time in 2023-2024, with 1 title across 2 tournaments — a clear sign that Nivedya is still ascending.

Nivedya has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Nivedya isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Nivedya brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
nivedya_s_nair.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nivedya S Nair, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 2 wins across 21 entries in 9 tournaments. That is a 10% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Nivedya shows up to a tournament, Nivedya pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Nivedya saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2022 Midwest CRC in GD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Nivedya's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nivedya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Nivedya is 0-for-9. Zero titles in 9 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Nivedya and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Nivedya needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Nivedya has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Veronica Ornatsky (8 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in XD U17 — we hope Sri Raghuram Vasamsetti has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Nivedya's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Nivedya has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nivedya has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nivedya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Nivedya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 South Frisco ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 South Frisco ORC, where Nivedya entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Nivedya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 21 entries, and 19 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Nivedya looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Nivedya's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Nivedya has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Nivedya. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

niya_olivia_chen.roast:

This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Niya saga began at 2023 South ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2025 Fortius South CRC in GD U15 - Position 1-4, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Niya's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Niya would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 7 times Niya has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 7 times Niya has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Niya and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Niya needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Niya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Niya's texts. The go-to partner is Ananya Mishra (12 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Calum Lim has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Niya's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Niya has shown up 3 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Niya has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Niya has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Niya has 30 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Niya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U13, which means Niya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Niya entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Niya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 55 entries, and 54 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Niya looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Niya's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Niya has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Niya. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

niyati_tamerissi.name: Niyati Tamerissi
niyati_tamerissi.summary:
A Virginia competitor, Niyati Tamerissi has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 47 entries across 17 tournaments, Niyati is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

A true triple-threat, Niyati competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 19 different doubles partners over the years, Niyati adapts to anyone:
  • Medha Sivani Gudavalli (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Jonathan Cai (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Ruohan Joy Du (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #5)
  • Siddhanth Selvanathan (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Niyati has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Niyati has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Niyati brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
niyati_tamerissi.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Niyati Tamerissi, from Virginia, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Niyati has 47 career entries across 17 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Niyati saga began at 2022 NE CRC with 1 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Niyati plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 47 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Niyati has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Niyati's texts. The go-to partner is Medha Sivani Gudavalli (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Justin Dong has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Niyati has shown up 6 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Niyati is not just losing locally — Niyati is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Tamerissi family travels cross-country so Niyati can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Niyati has 27 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Niyati showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15, which means Niyati traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Niyati entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Niyati paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 47 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Niyati. Niyati just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Niyati has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Niyati. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Niyati: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

noah_chien.name: Noah Chien
noah_chien.summary:
Out of Maryland, Noah Chien is a Pan American Junior Championships silver medalist, earning a podium finish representing the USA against the best young players in the Western Hemisphere (BD U17). A top-2 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Noah in rarified international company.

Noah is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Noah is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 4 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior silver medalist (BD U17, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U17 BD) • Junior Nationals competitor (8 entries) • 5 ORC titles.

Noah has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 16 different players across the career:
  • Rithik Rasamsetti (current primary partner) — 8 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (8 total events in BD/U17, across 2 seasons)
  • Clarinda Jin (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (3 total events in XD)
  • Eshan Neog (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in BD
  • Ishi Reddy — 6 events, 1 title together in U17/XD

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 6 podium finishes across 3 tournaments. Noah is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Noah has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Noah has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seeded entries across the career, Noah consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 6 seasons and 8 titles, Noah has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
noah_chien.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Noah Chien, from Maryland, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Noah is a Pan American Junior Championships silver medalist (BD U17), which means Noah has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Noah's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a silver medal: it means Noah went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Noah represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 57 career entries and a 14% win rate, the Pan Am silver is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 57 entries worth of material.

The Noah saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 13th. The first title would not come until 2023 NE OLC (NVBC) in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Noah's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Noah experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Noah has reached 10 championship matches across this career. That is 10 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 10 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 NE CRC, where Noah entered 2 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U13, BD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (7 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Noah does own 8 actual titles, so it is not like Noah has never won anything. But 10 finals losses versus 8 wins means Noah is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Noah plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 4 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Noah can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Noah has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Noah's texts. The go-to partner is Rithik Rasamsetti (8 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U15 — we hope Alexander Gu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Noah entered 2 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Noah's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Noah and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Noah has shown up 8 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

One thing nobody can accuse Noah of is playing it safe. Noah has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Noah has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 8 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Noah has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Noah is not just losing locally — Noah is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 14% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chien family travels cross-country so Noah can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Noah has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Noah showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13, which means Noah traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Noah entered 3 events with results ranging from 6th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 24th, which is a polite way of saying Noah paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 8 titles to the name, 57 entries, and 49 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Noah looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Noah's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Noah has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Noah. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
