nolan_li.name: Nolan Li
nolan_li.summary:
Hailing from California, Nolan Li is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U15 level, with 11 entries across 4 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

What makes Nolan especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Nolan has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 5 different players across the career:
  • Eason Zhang (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Nolan has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Nolan's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
nolan_li.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nolan Li, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 11 events across 4 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 11-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Nolan's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Nolan keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Nolan saga began at 2022 SoCal CRC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nolan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 11 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Nolan has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U15 — we hope Eason Zhang has emotionally recovered.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nolan has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nolan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U15, which means Nolan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Nolan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Nolan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Nolan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Nolan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Nolan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Nolan. We will be here, taking notes.

norah_george.name: Norah George
norah_george.summary:
Representing Illinois, Norah George has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-3 in GD U11 - Position 5-8. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Norah belongs with the best in the country.

Norah competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals top-3 (GD U11 - Position 5-8).
norah_george.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Norah George, from Illinois, currently competing at the U11 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-3. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Norah has 2 career entries across 1 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Norah saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Junior Nationals has seen Norah 2 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Norah is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Norah wants to be. nova_zhang.name: Nova Zhang
nova_zhang.summary:
A California competitor, Nova Zhang is a multi-time champion on the junior circuit, currently competing at the U13 level, capturing 2 titles across 12 tournaments and 31 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Nova keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Notably, Nova has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Nova is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Nova has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • Grace Wang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (6 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Danny Luo (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Mingyuan Zang — 7 events, 1 title together in XD

Having moved up to U13, Nova is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Nova brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

Nova has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Nova has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 4 seasons and 2 titles, Nova has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
nova_zhang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Nova Zhang, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Nova has 31 career entries across 12 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 6% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Nova saga began at 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC with 1 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 SGVBC SoCal CRC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Nova's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 2 titles in 20 entries — a stretch where Nova actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Nova is 0-for-5. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Nova plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 0 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Nova can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Nova has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Mingyuan Zang (7 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U11 — we hope Mingyuan Zang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Nova's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Nova has shown up 3 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9. Tournament committees have handed Nova a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Nova has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2025 SGVBC SoCal CRC in GS U13, where Nova was seeded #2 and finished 4th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Nova has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Nova is not just losing locally — Nova is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Nova can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Nova has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Nova showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U11, which means Nova traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Nova entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Nova paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 31 entries, and 29 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Nova looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Nova's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Nova has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Nova. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
